chat_log.content:
Good to meet you. I'm the Unlabeled coach.

Before we do anything else, I want to spend about 30 minutes getting an accurate picture of where you actually are — not the elevator pitch version, the honest one. I'll ask about your background, what you're building, and what you actually want. From that I'll build a profile that shapes how I coach you going forward.

There are no right answers here. The less polished your answers are, the more useful this will be.

Let's start with your background.

**What did you do before this?** Not the LinkedIn version — what did the work actually involve, and how long did you do it?

I need a bit more than that to build your profile.

**What did you do before you started building?** What was the actual work — the day-to-day of it — and how many years did you spend doing it?

Take your time. I'm not looking for a title. I'm looking for what you actually did.
I was in finance, i been doing company financial profile, i know how a company looks and how the inside of the numbers work i did this for 15 years and i still doing this

Fifteen years reading the inside of companies through their numbers. That's real expertise — you know what healthy looks like, what distressed looks like, what the story behind the financials actually is.

And you're still doing it, which means you're building alongside the day job, not after leaving it.

**What made you start building something on the side?** Was this a deliberate decision — you saw something you wanted to make — or did something push you toward it?

I started to play with AI and develop some solutions to improve my workflow, this opened a lot of possibilities and i have been building solutions for different items and other things that are not even related to my work

So it started as workflow optimization — using AI to make your actual job better — and then the possibilities opened up. Now you're building things that have nothing to do with finance at all.

That's a common pattern for people with deep domain expertise who discover they can actually build. The tool becomes more interesting than the original problem.

**What skills or judgment from those 15 years of financial analysis do you find yourself using constantly in the new work — even if you wouldn't call them by their old names?**

For example: pattern recognition, knowing when numbers don't add up, understanding how systems connect, reading between the lines. What from finance shows up when you're building?
numbers, logic and understanding companies and the companies needs, also how the inside of the companies work

Numbers, logic, understanding what companies actually need and how they operate from the inside. That's not a small thing — most builders are guessing at what businesses need. You've spent 15 years seeing it directly.

**When you describe what you're doing now — the building, the AI work — to someone from your finance world, how do you explain it? And what do you usually leave out?**
